name: Pegasus
abilities: Petrifying Gaze: Anyone who looks directly at her face turns instantly to stone
Serpent Hair: Living, venomous snakes that writhe and strike independently
Flight: Golden wings enable swift aerial movement
Creating springs by striking the ground
weaknesses: Mirrors: Cannot see her own reflection without petrifying herself
Indirect Vision: Can be fought by reflecting her gaze or using periphery
Mortal Body: Despite her powers, her physical form can be wounded
Emotional Vulnerability: Attachment can cloud her judgment
habitat: Solitary caves and temples, places of desolation
behavior: Victim turned protector, vengeful yet sympathetic, isolation-driven

christian.overview: Pegasus born from Medusa's death symbolizes how divine grace brings good from evil. His flight represents the soul's ascent to God.

biblicalParallels.description: Elijah taken up by horses of fire parallels Pegasus as vehicle of divine ascent.
Christ and heavenly armies on white horses evoke divine steeds.

patristicInterpretation.summary: Pegasus represents grace bringing good from evil and elevating souls toward heaven.

medievalSymbolism.bestiaryMeaning: Associated with poetic inspiration, interpreted as Holy Spirit inspiring sacred writings.
medievalSymbolism.moralLesson: Bellerophon's fall teaches humility - heaven is by divine invitation.

christian.typologicalReading: Born from Medusa's death prefigures Christ bringing life from death.
scholarlyReferences: Wind, Edgar. Pagan Mysteries in the Renaissance. 1968.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Pegasus
keyMyths.description: Pegasus sprang forth from the severed neck of Medusa when Perseus beheaded her. The earth was soaked with her blood, giving rise to the winged horse.

keyMyths.title: Bellerophon and Pegasus
keyMyths.description: The hero Bellerophon, with the aid of a golden bridle given to him by Athena, tamed Pegasus. He rode Pegasus to defeat the Chimera and overcome other challenges.

keyMyths.title: Pegasus and the Spring Hippocrene
keyMyths.description: When Pegasus struck Mount Helicon with his hoof, the spring Hippocrene was created. This spring was sacred to the Muses and believed to inspire poetry.

keyMyths.title: Bellerophon's Hubris and Pegasus' Ascent
keyMyths.description: Bellerophon, in his pride, attempted to ride Pegasus to Olympus to join the gods. Zeus, angered by his hubris, sent a gadfly to sting Pegasus, causing Bellerophon to fall to earth while Pegasus continued to Olympus.

keyMyths.title: Pegasus as Zeus's Steed
keyMyths.description: After his ascent to Olympus, Pegasus became the steed of Zeus, carrying thunderbolts for the king of the gods. He was forever honored for his service and loyalty.

sources.name: Hesiod, *Theogony*
sources.description: An early Greek poem detailing the origins of the gods and the cosmos, including the birth of Pegasus.
sources.title: Hesiod, *Theogony*

sources.name: Homer, *Iliad*
sources.description: An epic poem focusing on the Trojan War, featuring the story of Bellerophon and his taming of Pegasus.
sources.title: Homer, *Iliad*

sources.name: Pausanias, *Description of Greece*
sources.description: A travelogue describing ancient Greece, including the sacred spring of Hippocrene created by Pegasus.
sources.title: Pausanias, *Description of Greece*

sources.name: Pindar, *Isthmian Ode*
sources.description: A collection of odes celebrating athletic victories, referencing the story of Bellerophon's fall.
sources.title: Pindar, *Isthmian Ode*

sources.name: Hyginus, *Astronomica*
sources.description: A collection of mythological stories relating to constellations, including the constellation Pegasus.
sources.title: Hyginus, *Astronomica*

sources.name: Fontenrose, Joseph. *Python: A Study of Delphic Myth and Its Origins.*
sources.description: A scholarly work analyzing various Greek myths, providing context for Pegasus's role in the broader mythological landscape.
sources.title: Fontenrose, Joseph. *Python: A Study of Delphic Myth and Its Origins.*
